sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Low-risk mental health presentations involve distress or functional impairment without immediate risk of suicide, violence, psychosis, or inability to care for self. Examples include adjustment reactions, situational stress, and mild anxiety or depressive symptoms.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Anxiety, irritability, low mood, or sleep disturbance
- Difficulty concentrating or making decisions
- Mild somatic complaints such as headache or fatigue
- Usually oriented, cooperative, and able to engage in discussion

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Exclude immediate suicide risk, aggression, psychosis, severe intoxication, or serious organic illness
- Use a respectful, non-judgemental approach and assess supports, current stressors, and immediate safety
- Capacity, consent, and confidentiality still matter in low-risk presentations
- Sedation is not routine care for low-risk presentations

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Provide reassurance, active listening, and a calm environment where possible
- Primary Survey and focused Vital Sign Survey, including BGL where relevant
- Encourage practical supports and appropriate follow-up pathways such as local mental health services, GP review, EAP, or trusted supports
- Escalate if risk is uncertain, the patient cannot be safely left in place, or the presentation is deteriorating

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Sedating medication should only be considered if the situation has changed and the patient is now an immediate danger to themselves or others, or there is another clear clinical indication within scope
- If escalation is required, reassess whether the patient now fits a high-risk mental health or medical presentation and manage accordingly

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Document the risk assessment, supports offered, safety-net advice, and agreed follow-up
- Respect patient autonomy and confidentiality wherever possible
